key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC eutectic interface, together with nanoscale interlamellar confinement and local Ni enrichment, enables plastic co-deformation and suppresses premature cracking in an ultrafine Cr-Ni alloy.
engineering_relevance: The work suggests a microstructure-design route for strong, ductile additively manufactured alloys by tuning interface spacing, chemistry, and orientation to promote load sharing across phases.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create a lamellar Cr-Ni eutectic microstructure (~450 nm spacing)
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM to resolve interface structure, orientation relationship, and interfacial chemistry
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension with correlative TEM to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: The abstract reports high flow stresses with large plastic strains without cracking, supported by correlative microscopy showing dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear/reorientation, and later dislocation activity in the BCC phase.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated on a specific additively manufactured ultrafine eutectic, so transferability to different compositions, spacings, or service temperatures may be limited.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
